title: FFI (Foreign Function Interface)
objectives: Call native C libraries directly from Dart

sections.markdown: dart:ffi lets Dart call C functions and use C data structures directly — no message serialization. It's ideal for using existing C/C++/Rust libraries (crypto, image codecs, ML, SQLite).

Use FFI when you need to call a C library or move lots of data cheaply (e.g., sqflite's successors, image processing). Use platform channels when you need platform services written in Kotlin/Swift. FFI is synchronous, so still offload heavy work to an isolate.
Leaking native memory: memory you malloc in C (via ffi) must be freed (calloc/free from package:ffi). Forgetting to free or mismatching struct layouts causes leaks and crashes.
Wrap FFI in a safe Dart API, run heavy calls in an isolate, and use ffigen to auto-generate bindings from C headers instead of hand-writing signatures.

flashcards.back: Calling C ABI functions/structs directly from Dart without serialization.
FFI calls C libraries directly (sync, no serialize); channels call platform code (Kotlin/Swift) asynchronously with serialization.
Native memory you allocate must be freed; mismatched struct layouts crash.

quiz.explain: FFI binds directly to C ABI libraries.

quiz.q: Compared to channels, FFI calls are…
quiz.explain: FFI is a direct synchronous call with no message codec.
You manage native memory and layouts yourself.

challenges.text: Call a C add(int,int) from a prebuilt shared library.
Pass and read a C struct via ffi, freeing memory correctly.
Generate bindings for a small C library with ffigen.
Wrap a Rust image-processing function and run it off the main isolate.

interviewQuestions: What is dart:ffi and when use it?
How do you manage native memory with FFI?
Is FFI synchronous? How avoid blocking the UI?
